title: Stress test reveals our resilient financial system
full_text:
The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) quietly passed a milestone in 2026. For the first time, the value of assets under APRA’s regulation surpassed $10 trillion.
That number captures the strength and scale of Australia’s banking system, the superannuation pool (including 23 million APRA-regulated member accounts worth approximately $3 trillion) and insurance company balance sheets. Behind that figure are the everyday financial arrangements of millions of Australians – wages moving through bank accounts, savings, loans, insurance policies that help people recover from loss, and superannuation savings that support retirement.
Surpassing $10 trillion in assets under regulation is not, in itself, a cause for celebration. But the continued growth of assets under APRA’s regulation underscores the value of a robust and balanced prudential framework that keeps pace with change. As Australia’s prudential regulator, our mandate is to provide Australians with a safe and stable financial system – an objective that is critical to the community’s financial wellbeing and the growth of the economy.
When the financial system is disrupted, the effects can be felt quickly, in household budgets, business confidence, retirement savings and the capacity of communities to absorb economic shocks.
Strong prudential settings help ensure banks, insurers and superannuation funds can keep providing essential services through good times and bad. Australia’s financial system remains resilient and well capitalised, underpinned by strong prudential settings and institutions that have demonstrated their ability to withstand severe shocks.
But resilience is not static. As banks, super funds, insurers and financial markets become more interconnected through funding relationships, asset holdings and shared service providers, shocks can move further and faster through our financial system.
This is why APRA is increasingly focused not only on the resilience of individual entities, but also the resilience of the broader financial system.
To better understand how this growing interconnectedness could affect Australia’s financial system, APRA recently conducted its inaugural System Risk Stress Test (SRST).
APRA’s SRST involved the four major banks and six large superannuation funds. It examined how a severe but plausible shock could affect the Australian financial system, with a specific focus on linkages between super funds and banks.
For banks, the SRST included liquidity pressures exceeding any experienced by large Australian banks over the past 50 years. The stress applied to superannuation funds was similarly severe, with member withdrawals and switching significantly surpassing levels observed during Covid-19. All the banks and superannuation funds participating in this stress test withstood the shock resulting from the severe SRST scenario and rebuilt liquidity over time.
This resilience was underpinned by strong and usable bank liquidity buffers, access to central bank facilities, and superannuation funds’ ability to raise liquidity from international listed asset markets.
These findings reinforce the value of strong capital, liquidity, and prudential safeguards in helping the financial system absorb shocks while continuing to provide critical services to households and businesses in times of stress.
The exercise also underscored the systemic role of superannuation funds. In stress, their actions can either intensify pressure or support resilience across the system. That reinforces the importance of strong investment governance, sound liquidity risk management and robust valuation practices.
The superannuation industry is increasing in systemic importance because of its growing scale, interconnections and influence on household wealth and real economy outcomes. Assets in APRA-regulated superannuation funds grew from $1.3 trillion in 2015 to $3.2 trillion by the end of 2025. Superannuation now accounts for almost half of the financial assets of households in Australia, and on current trends the superannuation system is on track to match and in time exceed the scale of the banking sector. As the sector has expanded, its importance to the rest of the financial system, particularly banks, has also grown. Superannuation funds hold around 40 per cent of banks’ domestic short-term debt, and a similar share of their equity. They also rely on banks for payments, foreign exchange, and custodial services, and have deep connections with insurers through the insurance benefits provided to members.
If not addressed, some vulnerabilities could increase as the superannuation sector continues to grow and mature. Decisions by a small number of large funds could have outsized and more consequential effects across the system.
Insights from the inaugural SRST will inform APRA’s core supervisory activities and policy priorities. The SRST should give Australians confidence that the foundations of the system are sound, even in a more uncertain and interconnected world. But resilience must be maintained, not assumed.
As Australia’s financial system evolves, APRA will continue its stress testing program and work with regulated entities to strengthen preparedness, so the financial system can continue to serve Australians through changing economic conditions.
Australia has gone more than three decades without a deep recession, and the last significant bank failure was in 1991, so it’s easy to think this is Australia’s default economic state.
Although the strength and stability of Australia’s financial system, built up over decades, gives us confidence about our ability to withstand adversity, this can’t be taken for granted. John Lonsdale is chair of the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority
